title: Imported hardware drives the system
body: Nigeria's digital ecosystem depends overwhelmingly on imported devices, with negligible domestic electronics manufacturing capacity.

title: Rapid adoption multiplies device turnover
body: 148.2 million internet subscribers and rising data demand keep expanding the volume of devices moving through homes, schools, offices, and markets.

title: Second-hand access extends fragile lifespans
body: Because fewer than 20% of Nigerians can afford new electronics, used devices circulate widely and often arrive with limited remaining life.

title: Repair markets absorb what formal systems cannot
body: Large volumes of electronics reach Nigeria through ports, markets, and traders before becoming uneconomical to repair.

title: Informal recovery becomes the default
body: Without recycling infrastructure at scale, informal workers dismantle, sort, burn, and strip devices to recover saleable materials.

title: Toxic exposure settles into communities
body: The waste burden remains in Nigeria through contaminated air, soil, and groundwater, with long-term health consequences for workers, residents, and children.
